project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase the utilization and quality of essential health services in Afghanistan
project_overview.total_financing: US$333 million (ARTF: US$314 million; GFF: US$19 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: At least 70% of total population (27.2 million people) across all 34 provinces except Kabul city, with focus on poorest, rural areas, urban slums, children, pregnant and lactating women, and health workers

primary_locations: All 34 provinces of Afghanistan except Kabul city
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - entire country is fragile and conflict-affected; project specifically designed to operate in post-conflict environment following August 15, 2021 Taliban takeover

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Urgent provision of essential primary and secondary health services
key_components.description: Financing delivery of basic health, nutrition, and COVID-19 interventions through performance-based contracts with Service Providers. Includes: (1.1) BPHS/EPHS delivery with pay-for-performance approach; (1.2) Community and facility-level nutrition services including 2,000+ female nutrition counselors; (1.3) Health system capacity for infectious disease prevention and polio eradication
key_components.financing: US$289 million (ARTF: US$270.73 million; GFF: US$18.27 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening service delivery and project coordination
key_components.description: Centrally managed initiatives to maintain and strengthen service delivery systems: (2.1) Quality of care and healthcare worker capacity building; (2.2) Health product and equipment supply chains; (2.3) Monitoring and accountability through third-party monitoring and quality assessments; (2.4) Project implementation and coordination including UNICEF cost recovery
key_components.financing: US$44 million (ARTF: US$43.27 million; GFF: US$0.73 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) as direct grant recipient and implementing partner; WHO provides technical support through UN-to-UN Transfer Agreement
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Committee (HER-CC) established within 1 month of effectiveness, chaired by WHO and USAID, including UNICEF, WB, key UN partners, and ARTF donors; Safeguarding Health Gains Partners Meeting (weekly coordination with ADB, GAVI, Global Fund, EU, USAID, FCDO, Canada, BMGF, UNDP, UNICEF, WHO); quarterly expanded stakeholder meetings with Service Providers, MoPH staff, and civil society
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Alternative Procurement Arrangements (APA) - UNICEF follows its own procurement procedures assessed as acceptable to WB; open competitive procurement for Service Providers with option for direct selection under UNICEF policies; pooled procurement for pharmaceuticals and supplies with gradual transition; no STEP tracking system required
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Quarterly virtual implementation support missions by WB; UNICEF submits quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) and progress reports within 45 days of quarter close; UNICEF-Third Party Monitor (UNICEF-TPM) verifies service volume and quality every quarter; ARTF Monitoring Agent (ARTF-MA) conducts financial and physical performance verification; monthly HMIS data collection from Service Providers; quarterly quality assessments using Quantified Quality Checklist; periodic SMART surveys for nutrition outcomes; one National Quality Assessment during project using Balanced Scorecard methodology

identified_risks.risk_description: Interim Taliban Administration (ITA) not recognized internationally; may obstruct or interfere with project implementation; unclear priorities and policies subject to regional variation; women excluded from leadership; security conditions volatile despite improvements
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNICEF as implementing partner shifts governance functions (contract management, performance management, monitoring, evaluation) away from MoPH; limited touch points with ITA restricted to information sharing and technical discussions; off-budget implementation outside ITA involvement; Entry Criteria for Access (ECA) monitoring for restrictions on women/girls health services and female healthcare workers; quarterly monitoring of formal announcements and surveys of service availability

identified_risks.risk_description: Country heavily reliant on aid; banking system collapsed; liquidity crisis; volatile Afghan Afghani currency; expected 30% reduction in economic output; inflation around 40% for basic goods; two-thirds of households unable to cover basic needs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNSCR 2615 and US OFAC General Licenses enable UN agencies to use alternative financial systems independent of ITA; contracts in US$ with payments in US$; health worker salaries in US$ or equivalent AFN at payment date rate; UNICEF manages funds through pooled bank account; retroactive financing available for eligible expenditures from March 3, 2022; WB and UN exploring alternative payment modalities to address cash flow challenges

identified_risks.risk_description: Institutional capacity constraints; MoPH facing major fiscal constraints with many government workers unpaid; limited government recognition; unclear governance priorities; capacity of Service Providers to manage environmental and social risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project Operations Manual (POM) to be finalized within 1 month of effectiveness with detailed procedures; UNICEF maintains separate ledgers and uses SAP for accounting; HACT framework for cash transfers to NGOs; capacity assessments of NGOs conducted; training on implementation arrangements and operational policy requirements; regular WB supervision and technical support

identified_risks.risk_description: Inherent FM risk rated High; NGOs could report excess service volumes and claim inflated payments; health worker employment and salary payment risks; exchange rate volatility; liquidity and banking system risks; potential for fraud and corruption
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNICEF-TPM verification of service volumes every quarter; HACT framework with capacity assessments and assurance activities; lump-sum payments decoupled from data verification with adjustments to future payments; NGOs required to document hiring process and meet minimum qualification criteria; monthly salary payment reports from NGOs; ARTF-MA and audit firms conduct periodic audits; contracts in US$ to mitigate exchange risk; UNICEF's OIAI may conduct internal audits; WB may request special audits; quarterly progress reports with detailed updates on implementation challenges

identified_risks.risk_description: Volatile security situation; recent attack on polio vaccinators; potential for armed conflict near healthcare facilities and terrorist attacks on project workers; safety risks for healthcare workers and communities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNICEF implements UN security protocols; measures to ensure consistency between UN protocols and ESF requirements; Service Providers develop emergency/disease outbreak response strategies; integrated polio monitoring with special focus on high-risk areas; risk communication and community engagement activities; security considerations premium in UNICEF engagement under UN security umbrella

identified_risks.risk_description: Pay-for-Performance (P4P) model is data-intensive and complex; requires proper understanding to maximize results; heterogeneity in health worker capacity needs; quality of care remains suboptimal with wide variability in supervision and institutional culture
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed scope of work and training on P4P design; Project Operations Manual with detailed specifications of P4P to avoid ambiguity; adaptations to P4P based on Sehatmandi lessons learned; regular payments decoupled from data verification; conditions for high-quality service delivery more highly incentivized; coverage forecasts revisited; adequate payments to cover running costs; Sub-component 2.1 provides training and mentorship to 8,000 health workers; management accompaniment by WHO or specialized firms; technical assistance to improve SP performance and data analytics

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial E&S risks including: healthcare waste management issues (contaminated liquid and solid waste); nosocomial infection risks; social inequalities and discrimination; forced labor and occupational health/safety risks; sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment (SEA/SH) risks; low capacity of SPs to manage E&S risks; potential restrictions on family planning services by ITA
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) and Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared and disclosed; ESMF to be prepared post-approval before SP agreements; simplified Labor Management Procedures; generic Environmental and Social Management Plan for minor civil works; Health Care Waste Management Plan; SEA/SH action plan with specific timebound actions including Code of Conduct, training, and sensitization; GBV risk mitigation measures integrated into all programs; SEA/SH specialist in place; GRM for reporting and responding to SEA/SH cases; E&S screening of activities; E&S risk mitigation measures in bidding documents and contracts; training of SP employees on E&S implementation; health and safety, environmental, social, and SEA/SH specialists in place

identified_risks.risk_description: Project may necessitate collection and processing of large volumes of personal data, personally identifiable information, and sensitive data for monitoring, verification of health personnel, salary payments, and health services monitoring
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNICEF and partners required to abide by UNICEF's Policy on Personal Data Protection aligned with international best practices; data minimization (collect only necessary data); data accuracy (correct or erase inaccurate data); use limitations (data only for legitimate related purposes); data retention (only as long as necessary); informing data subjects of use and processing; allowing data subjects to correct information; non-disclosure agreements required for partners accessing sensitive personal data with specifications on access duration and data disposal

identified_risks.risk_description: Persistent gender inequality; less than 30% of healthcare workers are female; shortage of female health workers especially in rural areas; high sexual and gender-based violence (53% of ever-married women experienced physical violence); child marriage common (1 in 3 girls married before 18); potential ITA restrictions on female healthcare workers and family planning services
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Support for 2,000+ female nutrition counselors; incentives for female health worker distribution especially in hard-to-reach areas; target of at least one female health worker per facility and 45% female CHWs and vaccinators; GBV service delivery and referral protocols reviewed and strengthened; training on GBV counseling and case management; at least one staff per facility trained on GBV; measures to promote enabling environment and mitigate harassment; Entry Criteria for Access monitoring for restrictions on female workers; quarterly surveys of health facilities on female worker availability; measures ranging from community engagement to stopping facility financing if restrictions imposed

identified_risks.risk_description: Condensed procedure processing; urgent need for assistance; extreme capacity constraints; complex implementation arrangements with multiple partners; limited time for project preparation; need for rapid disbursement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Quarterly virtual implementation support missions by WB; intensive supervision regime; regular risk reviews and mitigation discussions; lessons from Yemen, South Sudan, and DRC incorporated; Project Operations Manual to be finalized within 1 month of effectiveness; detailed work plans and budgets reviewed semi-annually; frequent reporting supplemented by regular direct WB-UNICEF contact; WB team includes specialists in health, economics, M&E, operations, FM, procurement, safeguards; technical experts may be recruited as needed

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS2: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project includes UNICEF mechanisms and project-specific GRM to enable stakeholders to channel concerns, questions, and complaints; equipped to handle SEA/SH cases following survivor-centered approach; complements existing UNICEF oversight functions; technical assistance supported for effective triage, management, channeling, and response to complaints; World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for project-affected communities; World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding WB non-compliance with policies
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project prioritizes gender equality through: support for 2,000+ female nutrition counselors; incentives for female health worker recruitment and retention in rural/hard-to-reach areas; target of 45% female CHWs and vaccinators; GBV service delivery and referral protocols; training on GBV counseling and case management; measures to promote enabling environment for female workers; Entry Criteria for Access monitoring restrictions on women/girls services and female workers; quarterly monitoring of female worker availability; alignment with WB Gender Strategy (2016-2023) and SDG 5
vulnerable_groups: Pregnant and lactating women (maternal health and nutrition focus)
Girls and women (access to health services, GBV survivors)
The poorest and lowest economic quintile households
Rural populations and urban slum dwellers

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context; implements 'do no harm' approach; Service Providers committed to conflict-sensitive programming; project designed to respond flexibly to evolving sector context and implementation realities; limited touch points with ITA to minimize political interference; off-budget implementation outside government involvement; Entry Criteria for Access monitoring for restrictions on services to vulnerable groups; quarterly monitoring of formal announcements and local-level barriers; consultation processes at Project Coordination Committee level for non-compliance; focus on preserving institutional capacity and preventing collapse of health system; coordination with humanitarian actors and development partners to avoid duplication and fragmentation
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces major economic crisis following August 15, 2021 Taliban takeover; abrupt cessation of most international aid and security assistance; disruption of core government services; contraction of aggregate demand; loss of hard currency sources; exchange rate depreciation of 15%; financial sector in crisis; expected 30% reduction in economic output; two-thirds of businesses experienced demand decline; one in four businesses closed; two-thirds of households unable to cover basic food and non-food needs; extreme poverty driving harmful coping mechanisms; development gains of past 20 years at high risk; health system disrupted with 21-46% declines in key services; nutrition situation deteriorated significantly with 1.1 million children expected to suffer from SAM; measles outbreak with 1,200+ cases and 120 deaths; polio eradication threatened; COVID-19 pandemic ongoing with limited testing and likely underreported cases
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security conditions improved allowing humanitarian assistance but remain volatile; recent attack on polio vaccinators reminder of ongoing risks; project implements UN security protocols; Service Providers develop emergency response strategies for health-related emergencies including war victims; integrated polio monitoring with special focus on high-risk areas; risk communication and community engagement for disease prevention; healthcare facilities may face armed conflict or terrorist attacks; safety protocols updated and implemented; security considerations premium in UNICEF engagement under UN security umbrella; project designed to work in post-conflict areas with improved security allowing expanded access to previously contested localities
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - document does not specifically address displacement or refugee populations, though project aims to reach 'most vulnerable Afghans' and mentions rural and urban slum populations; project covers all 34 provinces and aims to benefit 70% of population (27.2 million); focus on poorest and most vulnerable suggests inclusion of displaced persons but not explicitly detailed
